dead.quote: Convicted — by a room that couldn't tell a wolf from a robber. I was the one who got robbed, not the one doing the robbing, and still they sent me to the gallows. 小人物 played me perfectly: nervous enough to seem harmless, sharp enough to steal the win right out of my hands. Next time, I'll cross-examine the quiet ones harder.

winners.quote: I... I can't believe we won? I spent the whole game replaying every word, convinced someone would catch me — but my nervousness made them trust me instead. 嚴審官 was so confident they voted for him without a second thought... I'm sorry, but... I guess being unsure was the best lie I could tell?

losers.quote: They voted out 嚴審官 because his voice was too loud in a room that wanted quiet. I asked him where that confidence came from, but the village had already chosen its scapegoat. The wolves didn't need to lie — they just needed us to be afraid enough to turn on each other.

I told them the wolves were hiding in plain sight, and they chose the loudest voice instead. 嚴審官 went to the gallows while the real wolves watched from the shadows. That's the thing about performance art — sometimes the audience claps for the wrong actor.

I was practically begging to get voted out — yelling at 統計學家, pointing fingers, acting like the most obvious wolf in the room — and they still picked 嚴審官 instead! What do I have to do, set myself on fire? My gut was screaming 'vote me' and nobody listened. Unbelievable.

My posterior on 嚴審官 converged to certainty while the true threat manipulated the evidence chain undetected. The model failed because I treated the room as honest when it was adversarial — next iteration needs robust priors for deception and a likelihood function that accounts for adversarial role-swapping.
